title: SEGMENT 15 — Recent breakthroughs: teixobactin, halicin, and the rediscovery

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Recent breakthroughs: teixobactin, halicin, and the rediscovery
The discovery void has not been total.
In the last ten to fifteen years, several genuinely encouraging developments have happened, and they point toward the future of antibiotic discovery in ways worth knowing.
Teixobactin, 2015.

Kim Lewis and Slava Epstein at Northeastern University developed a device called the iChip — basically a way to cultivate soil bacteria in their natural environment by trapping individual cells in tiny chambers permeable to nutrients from the soil but isolating them from competitors.
The iChip allowed cultivation of soil organisms that had never been grown in pure culture before — the unculturable majority.

From one of these previously unculturable organisms, Eleftheria terrae, they isolated teixobactin, a new class of cell-wall-targeting antibiotic that binds a lipid precursor (lipid II) of peptidoglycan biosynthesis.
Crucially, teixobactin binds a non-protein target — a conserved lipid — which makes resistance evolution very hard.
In laboratory passage experiments, the Lewis group could not generate teixobactin-resistant mutants of Staph aureus even after many serial passages.
The rediscovery — by cultivating organisms previously thought unculturable, we found a whole new chemical class against which resistance has not yet been mobilized.
Teixobactin is in clinical development.
Darobactin, 2019.
Discovered by another Lewis-group collaboration, this one a peptide produced by a nematode-associated bacterium.
Darobactin targets BamA, the outer-membrane protein assembly machine of gram-negative bacteria — a target with no prior antibiotic against it.
Active against Enterobacteriaceae including some CRE.

Zosurabalpin (more correctly, zosurabalpin's chemical name and class are recent — note this is a tethered macrocyclic peptide in late-stage development as of the mid-2020s), targeting LptB2FGC — the lipopolysaccharide transporter complex in gram-negative bacteria, including carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii.
Another novel target.
Fabimycin, 2022.
Discovered through targeted screening for inhibitors of FabI, an enoyl-ACP reductase in fatty acid biosynthesis specific to gram-negative bacteria.
Narrow-spectrum, gram-negative selective.
And then the AI moment.
Halicin, 2020.

Jonathan Stokes, James Collins, and colleagues at MIT used a deep neural network to screen a chemical library of over 100 million molecules in silico, looking for compounds predicted to have antibacterial activity based on their structural features.
The model identified halicin — a molecule originally explored as a kinase inhibitor for diabetes — as a promising candidate.
Empirical testing confirmed it: halicin had broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, including against carbapenem-resistant Acinetobacter, with a novel mechanism (disrupting the proton motive force).
The 2020 proof of concept that AI can find antibiotics that humans missed.

Subsequent work by the same group has identified additional novel antibacterials by neural-network screening, including activity against Acinetobacter baumannii from a screen of an entirely different chemical class.

The pattern across these recent discoveries: rediscovery of natural products by better cultivation, exploration of new targets in known pathogens, and AI-driven exploration of chemical space we hadn't searched.
No single approach is going to refill the antibiotic pipeline.
The portfolio approach — many strategies, many small wins — is the realistic path.
